original_abstract: Skeletal editing has emerged as a transformative paradigm in chemical synthesis, enabling the precise, atom-level reorganization of molecular frameworks. This review provides a comprehensive examination of recent breakthroughs in single-atom insertions, deletions, and transmutations within (hetero)cyclic systems. By detailing the development of these cutting-edge strategies, we highlight how late-stage skeletal logic is reshaping the way chemists approach molecular design and structural diversification.
humanized_abstract: Originally documented by Brussee and co-workers almost three decades ago, skeletal editing (SE) as a contemporary synthesis tool has only recently come of age as a formal strategy in contemporary synthesis. As the individual practice of manipulating a molecule at the level of individual atoms—regardless of the method used to make these alterations—this review surveys recent advancements in single-atom insertion, edit, and substitution operations in cyclic systems, including heterocycles. By focusing on recent advancements in these innovative approaches and describing the thinking that has evolved in the synthesis community as late-stage skeletal logic has increasingly begun to enter practice, we detail how late-stage skeletal logic has started to redefine contemporary strategies for design and structural diversity in modern chemistry.
